segments.s679-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction here meaning “when.” بندوی خراد appears as a personal name (Banduy Khurad) rather than a common noun phrase; لشکر is the noun “army/host” and فروز is an epithet/adjective following the noun (typical Persian order) meaning “bright/glorious,” forming an epithet “bright/glorious of the host.”
segments.s679-g74: چو again means “when.” نستود is the simple past 3rd‑person transitive “praised” (verb before its object here by poetic inversion). لشکرکش is a compound noun used as a title/epithet (“army‑mover/warrior, invader/campaigner”) and نیوسوز functions as a proper name (Niyusuz) attached to that title as the thing/person being praised.
segments.s679-g75: تلی is the subject “mound/hillock.” بود is the past copula “was.” پر here is an adjectival phrase “full (of)” followed by سبزه “greenery/green growth” (so پر سبزه = “full of green”). وجای is the conjunction و fused to جای (“and/place”), linking an appositive or additional description, and سور is the noun “feast/revelry,” so the whole phrase describes the mound as a green place for a feast.
segments.s679-g76: سپه را is the definite direct object marked by را (“the army”). همی‌دید is an imperfect/progressive past form meaning “kept seeing” / “saw” (here rendered “saw/was seeing”), and خسرو is the subject that follows the verb (inverted word order for poetic style). ز دور is a prepositional phrase with ز “from” and دور “afar/at a distance,” meaning “from afar.”
segments.s679-g9: بجای کسی نیست is an existential negation: بجای 'in place of/for' + کسی 'anyone' + نیست 'does not exist'. ما را سپاس places 'ما' (us) before the object marker را and the noun سپاس 'thanks' — here meaning 'thanks given to us' (i.e. 'no one gives us thanks'). Word order is topical/poetic: the negated existence (there is no one) comes before the object phrase.
segments.s679-g10: وگر چند هستیم نیکی شناس is conditional/contrastive: وگر 'if / but if' introduces a concessive idea. چند here means 'a few / some', هستیم is the 1st‑person plural present 'we are', and نیکی شناس is a nominal compound 'knower/recognizer of good' (شناس = 'knower' used predicatively). The clause says 'and if there are some, we are those who recognize goodness.'
segments.s679-g11: شمارا زما هیچ نیکی نبود is a past existential negation: شمارا 'to you' (object form) + زما 'from us' modifies نیکی 'kindness/good deed', and نبود is the past form 'there was not'. The whole means 'there was no kindness from us toward you.'
segments.s679-g12: که introduces a result/reason clause 'such that/that', followed by the object phrase چندین غم ورنج 'many sorrows and pains' (چندین = several/many; غم = sorrow; ورنج is the fused conjunction + noun 'and pain'), and the impersonal/modal باید فزود 'must be added' (باید = 'it is necessary / must', فزود = 'to increase/add'). The verb comes at the end in classical order.
segments.s679-g13: نیاکان ما را پرستیده‌اید is a 2nd‑person perfect: پرستیده‌اید 'you have worshipped/revered'. نیاکان ما را (ancestors + our + object marker را) is the direct object: 'you have worshipped our ancestors.' The subject 'you' is the verb ending.
segments.s679-g14: بسی شور و تلخ جهان دیده‌اید: بسی functions as an intensifier 'much/very', شور و تلخ جهان is a coordinated noun phrase 'tumult and bitter (aspects of) the world', and دیده‌اید is the 2nd‑person perfect 'you have seen' — i.e. 'you have seen much turmoil and the bitter world.' Classical word order places the verb at the end.
segments.s679-g15: بخواهم گشادن یکی راز خویش uses بخواهم (present/subjunctive form of 'to want') + the infinitive گشادن 'to open/reveal' to mean 'I wish to reveal'. یکی راز خویش = 'one/a certain secret of my own' (خویش + ش = possessive 'my/one's own'). The infinitive follows the desiderative بخواهم in classical style.
segments.s679-g16: نهان دارم از لشکر آواز خویش: نهان 'hidden' modifies دارم 'I have/keep' (present tense 'I keep hidden'), از لشکر 'from the army' marks the source/party from whom it is hidden, and آواز خویش 'my own voice/my proclamation' specifies that even by his voice he keeps it concealed. Word order places the verb (دارم) before its complements in this short clause.
segments.s679-g57: 'بدو گفت' is literal word order 'to him said' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت = past 'said'). 'گردوی' here stands as the noun/phrase being addressed or described; 'کاین خود گذشت' is a relative clause contracted from 'که این خود گذشته' = 'which itself has passed' (ک = archaic/clitic form of 'که', این = 'this/that', خود = 'itself', گذشت = past form 'has passed').
segments.s679-g58: This is an optative/jussive clause: 'گذشته همه باد گردد به دشت' = 'May all that is past become (be) to the plain.' باد here is the optative particle 'may', گرد(د) is subjunctive/present-form 'become' or 'be', and the phrase 'به دشت' = 'into/on the plain' marks the destination.
segments.s679-g59: This is a coordinated list of items linked by the conjunction و (and): 'توانایی و کام و گنج و سپاه' = 'power and desire and treasure and army.' The items are presented thematically as things that might tempt a man.
segments.s679-g60: The clause states a general truth: 'سر مرد بینا نپیچد ز راه' = literally 'the head of a discerning man does not turn from the path.' سر (here 'head' = metonymy for the person/mind), 'مرد بینا' = 'a clear-sighted/discerning man', نپیچد is the negative present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن ('does not turn/swerve'), and ز راه = 'from the way/path' (ز = poetic 'from'). The verb negation uses the prefix ن- plus the present form to express a general statement.
segments.s679-g61: 'بدین رزمگه' functions as a locative phrase 'in/at this battlefield' (بدین = 'in this' / 'by this' in older usage), followed by the temporal 'امشب' = 'tonight'. 'اندر مباش' is a prohibitive instruction: 'do not be/do not remain inside' (مباش = negative imperative 'do not be'). The overall order places location and time before the prohibitive verb for emphasis.
segments.s679-g62: This is a subordinate temporal clause introduced by تا ('until'): 'ممان تا شود گنج و لشکر به لاش' = 'do not linger/withhold until the treasure and the army become corpses.' ممان here is an admonition 'do not delay / do not withhold', تا + شود introduces the point at which the speaker warns the addressee not to wait, and به لاش = 'into/to corpses' (لاش = corpse).
segments.s679-g63: This clause begins with the subordinating 'که' = 'that/because' introducing the speaker's certainty: 'من بی‌گمانم' = 'I am without doubt / I am certain.' 'کزین راز ما' = 'from this secret of ours' (کزین = 'from this', راز = 'secret', ما = 'our/us'). The whole functions as the content clause of the prior remark.
segments.s679-g64: This phrase continues the thought: 'وزین ساختن در نهان سازما' should be read as 'و زین ساختن در نهان ساز ما' — i.e. 'and from making/constructing this in secret, (they) will contrive against us.' وزین is a contracted classical sequence (و + زین = 'and from this'); ساختن = 'to make/build/construct'; در نهان = 'in secret/hiddenly'; سازما here has the sense of 'to contrive/make' with the pronominal element tied to 'ما' (us). The whole expresses that secret construction will produce a device/plot concerning us.
segments.s679-g33: شما برنشینید is a 2nd‑person plural/polite imperative/present form (literary) meaning “you sit down” or “take your seats.” The preposition با precedes ساز جنگ: ساز (a musical instrument) + جنگ (war) form a genitival phrase 'the instruments of war' (i.e. war‑music or war‑instruments). Word order is Subject (شما) + verb (برنشینید) + prepositional phrase modifying the action.
segments.s679-g34: همه begins the clause as an indefinite distributive 'everyone/all.' گرز و خنجر are coordinated direct objects (mace and dagger). گرفته is a perfect participial/adjectival form 'having taken' or 'seized' and links to بچنگ, an older fused prepositional phrase بچنگ = به چنگ 'into the grasp / in hand.' The literal structure is 'everyone, mace and dagger having seized, (into) hand' = 'everyone takes mace and dagger in hand.'
segments.s679-g35: بران is a literary adverbial/relativizer here meaning 'upon that / thereon / at that (moment),' introducing the action. برنهادند is past tense (3rd‑person plural) 'they raised / they lifted up.' یکسر is used adverbially here ('all/at once') modifying برنهادند, and سپاه is the direct object 'army/host.' The sequence is adverbial (بران) + verb (برنهادند) + adverbial/quantifier (یکسر) + object (سپاه).
segments.s679-g36: که begins a subordinate clause of purpose/result 'so that / such that.' یک تن 'one person / not one man' is the indefinite noun phrase; نگردد is negative subjunctive/optative (ن‑ + گردد) meaning 'should not turn / should not become / should not stray.' زفرمان here is a fused prepositional unit 'ز + فرمان' = 'from the command' (i.e. away from the king's order). شاه is the genitive possessor 'the king.' Overall: 'so that not one person may turn away from the king's command.'
segments.s679-g37: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when.' خسرو is the subject (proper name). بیامد is past tense 'he came.' The phrase بپرده is a prepositional/locative unit 'ب + پرده' meaning 'in/behind the curtain' or 'in the veiled/curtained (place),' and سرای is here used in a verbal/performative sense (classical usage) meaning 'to sing/utter' (literary sense listed). The sequence gives a short temporal clause: 'When Khosrow came, (he) [بپرده] سرای' = 'he sang/appeared in the curtained hall.'
segments.s679-g38: زبیگانه is a compound where ز (from/of) + بیگانه (foreign/stranger) yields 'of the foreigner' or adjectival 'foreign.' مردم is 'the people.' بپردخت is a past form of پرداختن (here used in the sense of giving up, yielding, or moving aside) followed by جای 'place.' The whole clause reads 'the foreign people yielded/gave up the place' (i.e., they made room or stepped aside). Note: classical verbs like پرداختن have a range of senses; context determines that it means 'gave up/moved aside' here.
segments.s679-g39: بیاورد is a past tense verb (3rd‑person singular or narrative) 'he brought' or 'he caused to come.' گستهم and وبندوی are names/pronouns of persons (classical names); را is the definite object marker attaching to the coordinated object. The structure is Verb (بیاورد) + [names + را] marking the objects brought.
segments.s679-g40: جهاندیده is an epithet 'world‑seen / worldly‑wise / experienced' modifying one of the brought persons. The conjunction و links the next epithet. گرد here would be read as an adjective 'round / (classical adjective)' modifying گردوی; گردوی is an adjective/noun phrase 'of walnut / walnut‑colored' (i.e. 'walnut‑hued'). The final را is an object marker repeating/emphatic on the described pair. In short: two epithets in apposition describe the persons just brought: 'the worldly‑wise and the walnut‑hued.'
segments.s679-g25: همی is a narrative/adverbial particle here (continuous/emphatic). The phrase کودکی بی‌خرد is a predicate noun phrase 'a foolish child' and داندم is a 1st-person past/preterite verb 'I considered/perceived' that takes that phrase as its object (literally 'I perceived as a foolish child'). Word order is Subject (implied) → object phrase → verb, typical of classical Persian statements.
segments.s679-g26: This clause has a coordinated instrumental phrase introduced by the bound prepositional prefix بـ (here fused with گرز and شمشیر) followed by the 1st-person past verb ترساندم 'I frightened (made fear)'. In context بگرز و بشمشیر functions instrumentally ('with a mace and with a sword') and ترساندم is a transitive past action 'I frightened (him/them)'.
segments.s679-g27: نداند is a present/classical-form negative 'he does not know' (or 'he knows not'). It introduces a subordinate clause with که 'that' and the 1st-person subject من followed by the noun شب شبیخون 'night-raid' and the present/subjunctive کنم 'I do / I will do' — here کنم expresses the action 'I will carry out a night attack'.
segments.s679-g28: This clause ends with بیرون کنم 'I will put/drive out', where کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive used for a future or intended action. اندرون بیم ('the fear inside') is the direct object (fear that is within), and برزم is adverbial/locational or an older verbal form placing the action in the field/battle context (here serving to qualify the manner/place of the action). The whole means 'I will drive the inward fear out.'
segments.s679-g29: اگریار is a fused phrase (classical 'اگر یار' = 'if (you are) allies/friends'); باشید is 2nd-person plural imperative/subjunctive 'be' or 'become' (here 'be my allies'); بامن 'with me' is an oblique phrase and به جنگ 'in/for battle' marks the domain. Together they read 'If you are my allies with me in battle' (i.e. 'If you join me in battle').
segments.s679-g30: چو ... گردد is a temporal clause 'when ... becomes' (چو = when; گردد = becomes/happens). شب تیره 'the dark night' is the subject of the temporal clause, and نسازم درنگ is a negated verbal phrase where ن‑ is the negative and سازم (from ساختن) means 'I make/do' with درنگ 'delay' as its object — idiomatically 'I will make no delay' or 'I will not hesitate.'
segments.s679-g31: چو شوید is a temporal subordinate 'when (she/it) becomes' (شوید = become, subjunctive/episodic). بعنبر is an instrumental/ablative phrase 'with amber (ambergris / perfume)', and شب تیره روی is a poetic epithet 'dark‑faced night' or 'the dark‑faced one' (an epithet for the beloved); the noun phrase follows the verb in the typical classical order. The clause sets the condition/time for what follows.
segments.s679-g32: بیفشاند is a 3rd-person verb 'sheds'/'scatters' (present/future sense in narrative). این گیسوی مشکبوی is the direct object: این 'this', گیسوی 'lock of hair' (with the ezāfe implied), and مشکبوی an adjectival compound 'musk‑scented' postpositively modifying گیسو. Together: 'this musk‑scented tress scatters (its perfume).'
segments.s679-g41: همه کارزار شبیخون بگفت is an archaic compressed clause: همه (all/everyone) modifies کارزار (here ‘those engaged in the battle’), شبیخون is the shout/word (‘night-raid’ or surprise attack) and بگفت is the past tense verb ‘said/cried’. In epic Persian the 3rd-person past form بگفت can stand with a plural subject; word order is topical (همه کارزار first) and then the reported cry.
segments.s679-g42: که با او مگر یار باشند و جفت is a subordinate clause introduced by که (‘that’ / ‘so that’). با او = ‘with him’. مگر here is an archaic particle expressing a wish/qualification (roughly ‘perhaps’ / ‘that’ — none of the listed senses exactly match), یار ‘companion’ is the object, باشند is the 3rd-person plural subjunctive/present (they should be), and جفت ‘mate/pair’ parallels یار. The clause expresses purpose/hope that companions and mates be present with him.
segments.s679-g43: بدو گفت گستهم کای شهریار is organised as ‘to him said Gostahem “O king …”’. بدو (‘to him’) is an indirect-object clitic phrase placed before the verb, گفت here functions as the reporting verb ‘said’ (the senses listed in the input do not match its use as a past-tense verb), گستهم is the speaker’s name (Gostahem) inserted after the verb, and کای شهریار is a vocative ‘O king’. This word order (IO – verb – speaker – vocative) is typical in classical epic diction.
segments.s679-g44: چرایی چنین ایمن از روزگار is a noun question/phrase: چرایی ‘the reason/why’ heads the clause. چنین ‘so/thus’ modifies ایمن (‘safe/protected’), and ایمن takes the preposition از + روزگار ‘from fate/the vicissitudes of time’. The whole phrase asks for the reason you are so protected from (or exempted from) the fortunes of life.
segments.s679-g45: تو با لشکر اکنون شبیخون کنی uses تو (you) as the subject, با لشکر ‘with the army’ as accompanying phrase, اکنون ‘now’ as temporal adverb, شبیخون the action ‘night-raid/attack’, and کنی the present-subjunctive/imperative-like form of کردن used here to exhort (‘you should make/launch’). Word order is standard: subject – adjuncts – verbal phrase.
segments.s679-g46: ز دلها مگر مهر بیرون کنی is a purpose/result clause: ز (poetic form of از) + دلها ‘from the hearts’. مگر again is an archaic particle here conveying a sense like ‘perhaps’/‘that’ (no exact listed sense), مهر ‘love/affection’ is the thing acted on, بیرون ‘out/away’ is an adverbial complement, and کنی is the subjunctive/optative form ‘you might cause/remove’. Grammatically this says ‘that/perhaps you will drive love out of hearts.’
segments.s679-g47: سپاه تو با لشکر دشمنند reads ‘your army’ (سپاه تو) followed by با لشکر ‘with the host/army’ and دشمنند ‘are enemies’. Here دشمنند is composed of the noun دشمن and the 3rd-person plural copula suffix -ند (= ‘are’). In older Persian the copula is attached in this way; semantically it says the two forces stand as enemies.
segments.s679-g48: ابا او همه یک دل ویک تنند should be read as ‘با او همه یک دل و یک تنند’ (with him they are all of one heart and one body). ابا here functions as a variant/orthographic form of با (with) or is a scribal prefix; او ‘him’ is the accompaniment, همه ‘all’ is the subject, یک دل و یک تن ‘one heart and one body’ is the predicate, and the final -ند is the 3rd-person plural copula ‘are’. The phrase states unity of feeling and body among them when it comes to him.
segments.s679-g49: ز یک‌سو ... ز یک‌سو is a parallel, poetic construction: ز + یک‌سو = 'from/on one side'; the two noun phrases follow (نبیره = 'grandson/descendant', نیا = 'ancestor'), giving a contrastive 'on one side X, on the other side Y'. Word order is preposition + locative phrase + noun.
segments.s679-g50: به مغز اندرون = a locative phrase 'in/into the inner core' (به used here in the sense 'towards/into' or 'in'); کی بود introduces an interrogative 'what/what sort of was' and بود is the past copula 'was'; کیمیا is the noun being asked about ('kīmiyā' = alchemy/secret). The clause asks rhetorically what mysterious quality resided in the inward breast.
segments.s679-g51: ازین سو = 'from this side' (از + این), followed by the noun برادر; وزان سو = 'and from that side' (وز = 'and from that'), followed by پدر. The two parallel prepositional locatives set up the opposing parties.
segments.s679-g52: همه پاک بسته is a compact predicate: همه 'all', پاک used adverbially 'entirely/purely', بسته 'bound/tied/together'; یک اندر دگر = 'one within the other' (an idiom meaning mutually or tightly joined). Together they say the parties were wholly bound together.
segments.s679-g53: پدر چون کند با پسر کارزار is a rhetorical question: پدر (subject) + چون کند 'how would/should [he] do' (چون = 'how', کند = present/habitual 'does/makes') + با پسر کارزار 'make war with the son'. The verb کند appears in the common classical Persian form for hypothetical/rhetorical questioning.
segments.s679-g54: بدین آروز کام دشمن مخار: بدین = به + این 'by/with this'; آروز کام = 'the wish/desire and its fulfilment' (آروز = desire, کام = fulfilment/desire); دشمن is the possessor 'the enemy's'. مخار is an irregular/uncertain form in the manuscript but functions here as a prohibitory/negative command meaning roughly 'do not grant/allow'. The whole clause warns: 'do not, by such longing, give the enemy their satisfaction.'
segments.s679-g55: نبایست گفت = impersonal modal phrase 'one must not / it ought not to be said' (نبایست = نبایستِ classical 'must not/ought not' with negation); این سخن 'these words/this speech'; با سپاه 'to/in front of the army' (با used as 'to' or 'in the presence of'). The clause prohibits speaking such words before the troops.
segments.s679-g56: چو گفتی کنون = conditional 'if/when you say now' (چو = 'if/when', گفتی an instance of saying/speech in this classical usage), followed by the result clause کار گردد تباه 'the affair/matter will become ruined' (کار 'affair/matter', گردد = 'becomes/happens', تباه = 'ruined'). This is a standard if → result construction.
segments.s679-g1: وزان is the fused poetic particle وَ ز آن (literally “and/from that”), here introducing a consequent moment. روی شد is an idiom: روی (face) with شد (‘became’) yields “his face appeared / he appeared” (verb before subject is common in older/poetic Persian). شهریار جوان is the subject phrase “the young king” placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s679-g2: چوبگذشت here is actually the contracted poetic spelling of چو بگذشت (“when/after he passed”); the clause means “when he passed.” شاد is an adverb/adjective meaning “happily.” از پل نهروان is a prepositional phrase “over/from the bridge of Nahrawan,” with از + place marking motion across/over in older usage.
segments.s679-g3: همه precedes the noun to mean “all/every.” مهتران (chiefs/elders) is the plural noun being called; را is the definite object marker marking them as the direct object. زلشکر is the fused phrase ز لشکر (“from the army/of the host”); بخواند is the past (3rd-singular) poetic form of خواندن used here in the sense “to call/summon.”
segments.s679-g4: سزاوار is an adjective meaning “deserving/fit” used as the noun phrase's descriptor. بر تخت شاهی is a prepositional phrase “upon the royal throne” (بر + تخت شاهی). نشاند is an archaic/perfective causative/past verb meaning “caused to sit / placed (on the throne),” with the object (the deserving man) understood from context.
segments.s679-g5: چنین گفت is a fixed opening meaning “thus he said” (چنین = thus/so, گفت = said — here the simple past of گفتن). کای is a vocative particle equivalent to “O!” addressing the addressees. نیکدل سروران is the vocative phrase “good‑hearted lords,” with the epithet نیکدل preceding the plural noun سروران.
segments.s679-g6: This segment lists two adjectival/participial epithets modifying سران (leaders): جهاندیده (“worldly‑wise, experienced in the world”) and کار کرده (“having done much; practiced/experienced”). The coordinating و links the two modifiers; word order is modifier(s) + noun, typical in Persian.
segments.s679-g7: بشاهی (“in/for kingship”) sets the topic. مرا (here tokenized as م) is the dative/possessive “for me” (i.e. ‘for my part’). این نخستین = “this first/foremost,” and سرست is an archaic predicate meaning “is head/foremost.” The order Topic + indirect object + demonstrative + predicate is a poetic topicalization.
segments.s679-g8: جز از = “except by/through,” آزمایش is “trial/ordeal,” and نه is the negation 'not.' اندرخورست is an older fused form of اندرخور است meaning “is fitting/appropriate.” Together the clause says that it is not fitting except by means of trial.
segments.s679-g65: بدان is an imperative clause: ب‑ is the verbal prefix and دان (from دانستن) is the verb 'know' — together 'بدان' = 'know that...'. The rest is a small clause 'لشکر اکنون آگهی رسید' (literal word order: to/of the army now information arrived) where رسید is the past 3rd‑person 'arrived/was received' and آگهی 'news/information' is what arrived; the prepositional/partitive phrase (بدان لشکر) links the news to that army.
segments.s679-g66: نباید is an impersonal/modal negative in the present/past sense 'must not / should not'. که introduces the following subordinate clause. سر بدشمن دهی is a verb phrase with دهی (present/subjunctive/epic present of دادن) 'you give' and سر 'head' as object; idiomatically 'سر ... دهی' = 'lose/surrender your head' or 'be killed/give your head to'. تو is the addressed 'you'.
segments.s679-g67: چو بشنید is a temporal subordinating phrase 'when he heard' (چو = 'when/on'), here introducing the main result. خسرو is the subject/name (the king). پسند آمدش is an impersonal/psychological predicate: پسند آمد literally 'it came as liking' = 'it pleased him', with آمدش 'came to him' indicating the experiencer.
segments.s679-g68: This is another psychological construction: به دل (dative for the heart/'to his heart') + رای او (his plan/counsel) as the theme, followed by سودمند آمدش 'it came (to him) as profitable/useful' — i.e. 'he found the counsel useful'. آمدش again is past 'came to him' used idiomatically.
segments.s679-g69: گزین کرد is a compound past 'he chose' (گزین = 'choose', کرد = auxiliary 'did/made' giving past). زان is a prepositional/partitive 'from that/from those', so زان سرکشان = 'from those chiefs/heads (or: the headstrong ones)'. مرد چند is a noun phrase 'a few men' with چند postposed meaning 'several/a few'.
segments.s679-g70: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. باشند is the 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of بودن 'to be' (here describing the men). The adjectives/nominal qualities that follow (برنیک و بد یارمند) describe those men: یارمند 'ally/companion' qualified by برنیک (positive quality) and وبد 'and bad' — together meaning roughly 'who would be good and trusty allies' (wording is compressed and poetic).
segments.s679-g71: چو at the start functions as a comparative 'such as / like'. The sequence names individuals (خرداد, برزین, گستهم) and ends with شیر 'lion' used as an epithet for گستهم ('Gostaham the Lion'). Word order is a simple list introduced by چو.
segments.s679-g72: This continues the comparison: چوشاپور (a name) followed by و 'and' and another چو 'as/like' introducing اندیان دلیر 'the brave Indians' (an attributive phrase). The repeated چو signals a series of likenesses — 'like Chushapur and like the brave Indians.'
segments.s679-g17: سخن گفتن is a verbal noun phrase meaning "speaking/words." من is the possessor: "my speech." بایرانیان follows as an indirect/target noun (dative/benefactive) meaning "to/for the Iranians." The whole phrase therefore reads literally as "the speaking/words of mine to the Iranians."
segments.s679-g18: نباید is an impersonal modal meaning "must not / should not." که introduces a subordinate clause whose verb is برند (3rd pl. subjunctive/ present of بردن) with بیرون as adverb "out/away" and ازمیان as a source "from (me / out of me)." Together: "they must not carry out (it) from me."
segments.s679-g19: کزین (from this) modifies the verbal noun گفتن (the telling); اندیشه من is the possessed noun "my thought/plan/intention." تباه is a predicative adjective placed before the verb that follows; the group is a nominal clause meaning "by this telling my plan (will be) ruined."
segments.s679-g20: شود is the auxiliary/lexical verb "become / will be" (3rd sing.). چون here introduces a temporal clause "when" and بگویند is a 3rd plural subjunctive/present (they say/announce) which reports what will happen; پیش سپاه means "in the presence of / before the army." So: "it will be ruined when they tell it before the host."
segments.s679-g21: من امشب is the subject/time adverbial "I, tonight." سگالیده‌ام is perfect (1st person singular) "I have set/harried/assailed" and here takes the following verbal noun تاختن ("to charge") as its complement: literally "I have set (them) to charge" or "I have made a charge."
segments.s679-g22: سپه را is the definite object marker construction "the army (as direct object)." به جنگ اندر is a locative/directional phrase meaning "into battle" (به + جنگ + archaic اندر = "into/within"), and انداختند is past 3rd plural "they threw/caused." Together: "they threw the army into battle / they put the host into the fight."
segments.s679-g23: که introduces a subordinate clause/explanation. بهرام را is a topicalized/accusative-marked name ("Bahram" as object/topic). دیده‌ام is perfect 1st sing. "I have seen," and در سخن is an adverbial phrase "in speech / in the tales/accounts," modifying دیده‌ام: "I have seen/heard of Bahram in the tales."
segments.s679-g24: سواریست is the copular nominal phrase (سواری + -ست = "is a rider/horseman"). What follows are attributive compounds: اسپ افگن is a two-word descriptive phrase ("steed/horse" + adjective/epithet افگن), and وکارکن joins a second epithet with و; syntactically these are predicates/modifiers of سواریست describing Bahram's appearance and activity.

sentences.s679-s37: When Banduy Khurad, bright of the host, and when Niyusuz, the army‑leader, were praised.
sentences.s679-s38: There was a mound full of green, a place for revelry; Khosrow saw the army from afar.
sentences.s679-s5: There is no one who gives us thanks; but if there are any, we are those who recognize goodness.
sentences.s679-s6: There was no kindness from us to you, such that one should add many sorrows and pains.
sentences.s679-s7: You have worshipped our ancestors; you have witnessed much turmoil and the bitter world.
sentences.s679-s8: I wish to reveal one secret of mine; I keep it hidden from the army and from my own voice.
sentences.s679-s29: He said to him, “Let this — which itself has passed — be past; may all that is gone be thrown onto the plain.”
sentences.s679-s30: Power, desire, treasure and army — a discerning man's head will not turn aside from the path.
sentences.s679-s31: Do not remain tonight in this battlefield; do not delay until the treasure and the army have become corpses.
sentences.s679-s32: For I am certain that from this our secret, and from making it in secret, a device against us will be formed.
sentences.s679-s17: You (pl.) sit down with the instruments of war, and everyone takes mace and dagger in hand.
sentences.s679-s18: They at once raised the whole army, so that not a single man would turn away from the king's command.
sentences.s679-s19: When Khosrow came, (he) sang/uttered in the curtained hall; the foreign people yielded their place.
sentences.s679-s20: He brought Gostaham and Banduy — the worldly‑wise one and the walnut‑hued (one).
sentences.s679-s13: I took him for a foolish child and frightened him with the mace and the sword.
sentences.s679-s14: He does not know that I will make a night-raid; I will (in battle) drive the fear from within out.
sentences.s679-s15: If you will be my allies in battle, when the night grows dark I will make no delay.
sentences.s679-s16: When the dark‑faced one is anointed with amber, this musk‑scented lock of hair scatters (its scent).
sentences.s679-s21: All the fighters cried out “night-raid!”, saying that companions and mates should be with him.
sentences.s679-s22: Gostahem said to him, “O king, why are you so safe from the turns of fate?”
sentences.s679-s23: You, with your army, should make a night-raid now; perhaps you will drive love out of hearts.
sentences.s679-s24: Your army is at odds with the host (they are enemies); yet with him they are all of one heart and one body.
sentences.s679-s25: On one side a grandson, on the other side an ancestor — what alchemy was in the inner core?
sentences.s679-s26: On this side a brother, and on that side a father; all pure and tightly bound, one within the other.
sentences.s679-s27: How can a father fight a battle with his son? By such a desire do not give the enemy their fulfilment.
sentences.s679-s28: One must not say this speech to the army; if you say it now, the matter will be ruined.
sentences.s679-s1: From that time the young king appeared; he happily crossed the bridge of Nahrawan.
sentences.s679-s2: He summoned all the chiefs from the army and set the deserving one upon the royal throne.
sentences.s679-s3: Thus he said, “O good‑hearted lords, worldly‑wise and practiced leaders...”
sentences.s679-s4: As for kingship, this is the foremost thing for me: it is not fitting except by trial.
sentences.s679-s33: Know that news of that army has now arrived; you must not give your head to the enemy.
sentences.s679-s34: When the king heard it, it pleased him; he found the counsel useful to his heart.
sentences.s679-s35: He selected from those chiefs a few men who would be good, trustworthy allies.
sentences.s679-s36: Such as Khordad, Barzin and Gostaham the Lion; like Chushapur and like the brave Indians.
sentences.s679-s9: My words to the Iranians must not be carried out (revealed).
sentences.s679-s10: For by this telling my plan will be ruined when they tell it before the army.
sentences.s679-s11: Tonight I have set (them) to charge; they hurled the host into battle.
sentences.s679-s12: That I have seen Bahram in the tales: he is a horseman, with a shining steed and a man of action.
